senses.definition: the act or process of using a pulley system, usually to lift or move heavy objects
examples: The pulleying of the heavy crate made the job easier for the workers.
Pulleying can reduce the effort needed when raising loads.

senses.definition: without a pulley or without use of a pulley system
examples: The pulleyless crane relies on manual lifting.
A pulleyless setup requires more effort.

senses.definition: plural of pulley; wheels with a grooved rim for guiding belts or ropes
examples: We replaced the damaged pulleys in the machine.
The belt runs smoothly over the pulleys.

senses.definition: a plural form of pullus in some contexts, or a term in biology referring to larvae of certain insects; not commonly used in everyday English
examples: In the older text, pulli are described as immature birds.
The term pulli appears in some zoological notes.

senses.definition: a speculative or obscure term; not widely recognized in standard English
examples: The manuscript mentions pullicat without defining it.

senses.definition: to form small lumps or to cause lobulation; a rare or obsolete term
examples: The mineral began to pullicate under pressure.
Certain materials pullicate when wet.

senses.definition: the act of applying force to draw something toward oneself or along a direction
examples: She is pulling the rope to lift the box.
Pulling steadily, the team moved the cart forward.

senses.definition: instances of pulling; acts of drawing something toward oneself
examples: Several pullings were needed to move the crate.
His pullings on the rope were synchronized with the others.

senses.definition: a hypothetical or misspelled form; not standard English
examples: The term pullisee does not appear in standard dictionaries.

senses.definition: a brand name or type of sleeping car or comfortable railway coach; also a surname or place name
examples: We booked a Pullman car for the overnight trip.
Pullman is also a common surname.

senses.definition: to furnish with or convert to a Pullman-style car or comfort; to make comfortable travel accommodations
examples: The railway company planned to pullmanize the fleet.
They aimed to pullmanize the sleeper cars.

senses.definition: plural of Pullman; sleeping cars or the brand
examples: The fleet includes several Pullmans.
Old Pullmans are considered collectible.

senses.definition: a surname or a rare place name; not common in modern usage
examples: Pullock is listed as a family name in some records.

senses.definition: pullorum disease, a bacterial infection in poultry, historically significant in veterinary medicine
examples: Pullorum disease was a major concern for hatcheries.
Vaccination reduced pullorum outbreaks.

senses.definition: the act of withdrawing or a detachment from a commitment or agreement
examples: The pullout of troops occurred last year.
They announced a pullout from the project.

senses.definition: a movable piece of a device used to extract or remove something
examples: The pullout drawer holds cutlery.
Slide the pullout handle to open.

senses.definition: plural of pullout; acts of withdrawing or movable drawers/sections
examples: The stadium has several pullouts for emergency exits.
Pullouts in the kitchen make extra space.

senses.definition: a sweater or jumper that is pulled over the head
examples: She wore a warm pullover on the chilly morning.
The pullover has a cozy knit.

senses.definition: plural of pullover; sweaters
examples: She has several pullovers in different colors.
Cotton pullovers are comfortable in cool weather.

senses.definition: third-person singular of pull; to exert force to draw something toward oneself or a direction
examples: He pulls the door open.
The cat pulls at the leash.

senses.definition: plural of pull; acts of pulling or a force that pulls
examples: Strong pulls can move heavy objects.
The pulls on the rope were synchronized.

senses.definition: a shovel designed to be pulled or used in a pulling motion; a specialized tool
examples: The worker used a pullshovel to move loose soil.
A pullshovel helps with digging in tight spaces.

senses.definition: causing or characterized by rapid spreading or growth; in biology, producing a slime or fluid
examples: The pullulant growth covered the surface quickly.
Pullulant substances can attract moisture.

senses.definition: to produce or create rapidly or in great numbers; to breed or proliferate
examples: The city pullulates with new businesses every year.
In spring, life seems to pullulate in the meadows.

senses.definition: past tense of pullulate; produced or proliferated rapidly
examples: The market pullulated after the release.
Rumors pullulated through the crowd.

senses.definition: third-person singular of pullulate; to proliferate or breed rapidly
examples: Small towns pullulate with new cafes each year.
The forest pullulates with life in spring.

senses.definition: present participle of pullulate; proliferating rapidly
examples: The city is pullulating with new shops.
Worms pullulating in the damp soil indicate moisture.
